roots.title: Introduction to SQL and Course Adjustments
roots.summary: The lecture introduces SQL as the new topic and outlines the course structure for the second half, indicating weekly topic changes. It reviews mid-semester survey feedback, noting that students are becoming comfortable with pandas, while gradient descent remains challenging. Fewer action items are identified this semester, with plans to release lecture materials sooner, enhance student experience through earlier discussion walkthroughs, and adjust office hours for better accessibility. Office hours will be distributed more evenly throughout the week, and upcoming slides will be more challenging, allowing more time for completion during lectures. Students can request grading accommodations for recent homeworks via a Google Form. Improvements based on mid-semester survey results include earlier release of discussion walkthroughs, ensuring a fair final exam, adjusting Slido difficulty, and spreading office hours throughout the week. The course will cover weekly machine learning topics, starting with logistic regression, followed by clustering algorithms and a lecture on large language models, focusing on their training and fine-tuning. This week emphasizes SQL with a homework assignment and lab, and students are encouraged to provide feedback through Ed and the mid-semester survey. Instructors are available via email and office hours, which are vital for support, and students should utilize resources like course notes, mini-lectures, and a debugging guide. Discussion sections will focus on worksheets with minimal lecture content, requiring students to watch mini lectures beforehand. Ed is the preferred platform for questions, and a Google form is available for accommodations. Support for illnesses and study strategies for the final exam are encouraged, emphasizing office hours and course resources.

children.title: Mid-Semester Survey Feedback Overview
children.summary: The course will focus on various tools and machine learning algorithms in the latter half of the semester, making it more engaging than the first part. Feedback from the Meet-Semester Survey, completed as part of Project A2, has been reviewed, highlighting students' comfort levels with different topics and identifying areas of confusion.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So we're going to go into different tools and we're going to go into different machine learning algorithms and so on and so forth up until the last week of the of the last week of the semester. So things are going to be a little bit more fun compared to the first half of data 100. So what we did previously was, if you remember, as part of Project A2, you have completed the Meet-Somester Survey. We actually went through all the responses and all the written feedback and all of the numerical feedback that you have given us. And I have tree slides as or forest slides as a summary of the things that I want to share with you. So there is one thing is that when we ask this is kind of close to after midterm and also by the time that you submitted A2, was that which topic you're most comfortable with, which topics do you think are confusing? children.title: Mid-Semester Survey Insights and Expectations
children.summary: Most students are becoming comfortable with pandas, which is expected as they will use it in future homework and projects. The mid-semester survey indicated that gradient descent remains a challenging topic, consistent with previous semesters. However, as the course progresses and more advanced topics are introduced, understanding is expected to improve.

At the time of the survey, people were struggling with great endocent. That is also expected. I also like that the mid semester survey in the past two semesters, exactly the same distribution, great endocent was the most challenging part right after the midterm. But it will get better. children.title: Mid-Semester Feedback and Action Items
children.summary: Feedback from the mid-semester survey indicates that there are fewer action items this semester due to smoother course operations. There are plans to release lecture materials sooner to aid students in preparing for office hours and discussions. Actions being taken to enhance student experience include releasing discussion walkthroughs earlier, ensuring a fair final exam, adjusting Slido difficulty, spreading office hours throughout the week, and addressing issues with written homework submissions.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: every kind of lecture on every week. There are some feedbacks that relate to some action items for us. So this kind of semester, we have less action items, because it looks like the decor is going smoothly compared to the previous semesters that we had. There was some comments about discussion, many lecture being really still a bit late, so we'll try to work on releasing those kind of sooner, so you can use that when you're going to office hours, when you're going to discussions. children.title: Office Hours Utilization and Adjustments
children.summary: Office hours are currently underutilized, prompting a plan to distribute them more evenly throughout the week for better accessibility. Additionally, the upcoming slide will be more challenging, and more time will be allocated during the lecture for students to complete it. Feedback from the mid-semester survey indicates actions being taken to enhance the student experience, including earlier release of discussion walkthroughs, ensuring a fair final exam, and addressing issues with written homework submissions.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: There was a request on distributing office hours a little bit more. In the week, right now this semester, what I'm seeing in terms of the number of tickets that we see online and also the number of students are coming to office hours in Warren Hall is that our office hours are not being utilized by you all. So utilize office hours, but we're also going to try to distribute a little bit more during the weeks so that we have... We have more coverage of all the times that you're hopefully needing some help from one of the TAs. There was an interesting set of comments about this slide. It can be more challenging. We're going to give you the try. So this slide is going to be a little bit more challenging. We're going to give you more time to complete this slide in the lecture. So meaning that we will stay more on each one of the slide questions. There was a comment and also a few people came to my office. Video: 1: lec20-demo.i ... - JupyterLab

children.title: Homework Accommodations and Machine Learning Topics
children.summary: Students can request grading accommodations for recent homeworks via a Google Form. Improvements based on mid-semester survey results include earlier release of discussion walkthroughs, ensuring a fair final exam, adjusting Slido difficulty, and spreading office hours throughout the week. The course will cover weekly machine learning topics, starting with logistic regression, followed by clustering algorithms and a lecture on large language models, focusing on their training and fine-tuning. This week emphasizes SQL with a homework assignment and lab, and students are encouraged to provide feedback through Ed and the mid-semester survey. Instructors are available via email and office hours, which are vital for support, and students should utilize resources like course notes, mini-lectures, and a debugging guide.

children.title: Homework Accommodations and Upcoming Machine Learning Topics
children.summary: Students who did not select pages for recent homeworks, particularly homework one and assignments five a and five b, can use a Google Form to notify instructors for grading accommodations. Improvements based on mid-semester survey results will include earlier release of discussion walkthroughs, ensuring a fair final exam, adjusting Slido difficulty, and spreading office hours throughout the week. The course will cover weekly machine learning topics starting with logistic regression, essential for understanding neural networks, followed by algorithms like clustering and a lecture on large language models, focusing on their training and fine-tuning.

children.title: Homework Submission Accommodations and Course Improvements
children.summary: Accommodations will be made for students who did not select pages for recent homeworks, specifically homework one and assignments five a and five b. A Google Form is available for students to notify instructors for grading. Additionally, improvements based on mid-semester survey results include releasing discussion walkthroughs earlier, ensuring a fair final exam, adjusting the difficulty of Slido, and spreading office hours throughout the week.

children.title: Homework Submission Accommodations and Course Improvements
children.summary: Due to concerns about written homework submissions, accommodations will be made for students who did not select pages for recent homeworks, specifically homework one and assignments five a and five b. A Google Form has been provided for students to notify the instructors for grading. Additionally, actions are being taken to improve the student experience based on mid-semester survey results, including releasing discussion walkthroughs earlier, ensuring a fair final exam, adjusting the difficulty of Slido, and spreading office hours across the week.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I heard they were concerned about written homework submission, meaning that they didn't select pages and they got zero and whatnot. That is, I believe homework one and then that is five a and five b. So if that's you, that is posted on And there's a Google Farm that you fill out. You let us know. We'll go ahead and grade it. children.title: Upcoming Topics in Machine Learning
children.summary: The course will cover various machine learning topics weekly, beginning with logistic regression, which is crucial for understanding neural networks. Subsequent topics will include additional ML algorithms such as clustering, and a dedicated lecture on large language models, focusing on their training and fine-tuning as relevant to the course.

children.title: Upcoming Topics in Machine Learning
children.summary: The course will cover more machine learning (ML) topics weekly, starting with logistic regression, which is essential for understanding neural networks. Following that, additional ML algorithms like clustering will be introduced. There will also be a lecture focusing on large language models, discussing aspects of their training and fine-tuning relevant to the course content.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Not surprisingly, people are interested in seeing more ML topics, so we will see more ML topics actually every week. In fact, we're going to see one of my most favorite ML algorithms. That's logistic regression, which is kind of the core and kind of the backbone of understanding neural networks. So, we're going to teach you logistic regression pretty soon. I believe next week is the Legislature Congressional week. And then we're gonna also go into some more ML algorithms such as clustering and whatnot. People also said that they're excited about large language models. There is one lecture or like half of a lecture talking about large language models and what part of large language model training and fine tuning and whatnot would you understand with your knowledge and data 100. children.title: Course Logistics and Resource Utilization
children.summary: This week focuses on SQL, with a homework assignment and a lab. Students are encouraged to provide feedback through Ed and the mid-semester survey. Instructors are available via email and office hours, which are emphasized as essential for student support. Networking with TAs is encouraged for future courses and graduate school. Students should utilize resources such as office hours, discussion sections, course notes, mini-lectures on the website, and a debugging guide for homework and projects. Effective communication is important for accommodations and feedback.

children.title: Course Logistics and Importance of Resources
children.summary: This week will focus on SQL, including a homework assignment and a lab. Students are encouraged to provide feedback through Ed and the mid-semester survey. Instructors are available via email and office hours for concerns, with office hours emphasized as a key resource for student support. Networking with TAs, selected for their expertise, is encouraged for future courses and graduate school. Students should utilize various resources such as office hours, discussion sections, course notes, mini-lectures on the website, and a debugging guide for homework and projects. Communication is vital for accommodations and feedback.

children.title: Course Logistics and SQL Coverage
children.summary: This week will focus on SQL, including a homework assignment and a lab. Students are encouraged to provide feedback through Ed and the mid-semester survey. Instructors are available via email and office hours for any concerns, and the student support team can help schedule meetings with TAs. Office hours are highlighted as a key resource for student support.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So we're gonna cover that in the last lecture up the semester and some students were excited about SQL. Yes, this week we're going to cover SQL, so we're going to see how that goes. Please feel free to reach out to us through Instructors email account. So there's data 100 data instructors at Berkeley.edu or come to our office hours. So if you have concerns, if you don't want to necessarily post things online or email, we're also happy to talk to you in office hours. Our student support team is also available to schedule one-on-one meetings with a student. If you want to discuss something with the TA's and you don't necessarily want to talk to instructors. children.title: Importance of Office Hours and Resources
children.summary: Students are encouraged to attend office hours to build networks with TAs, who are selected for their expertise. Networking with TAs can be beneficial for future courses and graduate school. Additionally, students are reminded to utilize various resources such as office hours, discussion sections, course notes, mini-lectures available on the website, and a debugging guide for homework and projects. Communication is emphasized for accommodations and feedback through forms linked at the end of assignments.

children.title: Course Logistics and Introduction to SQL
children.summary: Discussion sections will focus on worksheets with minimal lecture content, requiring students to watch mini lectures beforehand. Ed is the preferred platform for questions, and a Google form is available for accommodations. Support for illnesses and study strategies for the final exam are encouraged, emphasizing office hours and course resources. SQL is introduced as crucial for managing larger datasets, highlighting its relevance for future job and academic opportunities. Current data processing methods are insufficient for large datasets, indicating a need for new technologies beyond Pandas and DataFrames.

children.title: Course Logistics and SQL Introduction
children.summary: Discussion sections will focus on worksheets with minimal lecture content, requiring students to watch mini lectures beforehand. Ed is the preferred platform for questions, and a Google form is available for accommodations. Support for illnesses and study strategies for the final exam are encouraged, emphasizing office hours and course resources. SQL is introduced as crucial for managing larger datasets, highlighting its relevance for future job and academic opportunities. Current data processing methods are insufficient for large datasets, indicating a need for new technologies beyond Pandas and DataFrames.

children.title: Course Logistics, SQL Introduction, and Data Processing
children.summary: Discussion sections will involve minimal lecture content, focusing on worksheets, with students expected to watch mini lectures beforehand. Ed is the preferred platform for questions, and a Google form is available for accommodations. Support for illnesses and study strategies for the final exam is encouraged, with emphasis on office hours and course resources. The introduction to SQL highlights its importance for managing larger datasets and its relevance for future job and academic opportunities. Current data processing methods are deemed insufficient for large datasets, necessitating new technologies beyond Pandas and DataFrames for effective data management.

children.title: Discussion Sections and Course Resources Overview
children.summary: Discussion sections will focus on working through worksheets with minimal lecture content at the beginning. Students should watch mini lectures prior to discussions for a condensed summary of topics. Utilize Ed for questions instead of email, as it can generate responses based on historical data, which TAs will review for accuracy. A Google form is available for requesting accommodations or extensions. Office hours and various course resources, including discussion sections and course notes, are encouraged for student support.

If you're comfortable with the lecture and you watch lectures before you go to the discussion, That's also fine. So it is kind of a condensed summary of the lectures that are covered in that specific discussion. Um. Utilize Ed. If you have questions, don't email. Utilize Ed. Make a person Ed. children.title: Final Exam Preparation and Course Resources
children.summary: Students are encouraged to reach out for support regarding any illnesses or study strategies for the final exam. The final exam is being rigorously tested to ensure clarity and quality. Key resources include office hours, discussion sections, course notes, and mini-lectures available on the website. Students should utilize the debugging guide for homework and project errors and communicate any needs through the accommodations form.

We actually right after the midterm, we started writing the final exam and we're testing it rigorously. So hopefully the final exam is going to be really good and should be super clear and good to go when you're taking it. All right. children.title: Introduction to SQL and Databases
children.summary: The lecture introduces SQL, emphasizing its importance in handling larger datasets compared to smaller CSV or JSON files previously used in the course. It highlights the transition from Python and pandas to SQL, explaining the relevance of databases in real-life scenarios, including job and graduate school applications. The instructor aims to convey why learning SQL is essential for processing substantial amounts of data.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I'm going to start the topic of SQL. SQL is really interesting. So the first half of today's lecture, I'm going to actually talk about why we need to go through SQL and why we need to have databases and learn about databases. And kind of it is moving us away from pandas and Python programming that we have been doing before. So remember that I was here the first four lectures of Native 100 teaching Japan does this is going to be again me here teaching you a second programming language. But before I do that I will try to tell you why it's very important and why it's really useful in your life and in your job or grad school as well. previously, what you'd seen was that we would hand you a data in a CSV format or in JSON format or whatever, and then you would be able to read it and parse it into a data frame, and you would use that data frame in order to do any kind of processing that you had. Usually the data sets that we shared were kind of smaller in size compared to, for example, gigabytes and terabytes of data that you might need to deal with in real life scenarios. leaf_chunk.audio: I'm going to start the topic of SQL. SQL is really interesting. So the first half of today's lecture, I'm going to actually talk about why we need to go through SQL and why we need to have databases and learn about databases. And kind of it is moving us away from pandas and Python programming that we have been doing before. So remember that I was here the first four lectures of Native 100 teaching Japan does this is going to be again me here teaching you a second programming language. But before I do that I will try to tell you why it's very important and why it's really useful in your life and in your job or grad school as well. previously, what you'd seen was that we would hand you a data in a CSV format or in JSON format or whatever, and then you would be able to read it and parse it into a data frame, and you would use that data frame in order to do any kind of processing that you had. Usually the data sets that we shared were kind of smaller in size compared to, for example, gigabytes and terabytes of data that you might need to deal with in real life scenarios.

children.title: Data Processing and Technology Needs
children.summary: Current data processing methods are not scalable for large datasets, necessitating new mechanisms to handle dynamic data. Static data management is insufficient, and technologies must efficiently store, manage, and process changing datasets. While Pandas and DataFrames have been useful for certain tasks, more efficient programming languages will be introduced to better handle these requirements.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So therefore, what we have had so far is not going to be scalable and we need to have different new mechanisms in order to process large amounts of data. What you also saw every time it gave you a data, we did not update the data, right? So the data was the static. So that is very important for you to decide what kind of technology you want to use in order to load the data and read the data. So we had the static data that was the small, but then in real life scenario, we have the data that is changing. So we have the snapshots with different frequency of the data and then the data set is going to be large. of those cases, we really want to move towards a technology that is really efficient in storing the data, and it can efficiently manage the data, and it can efficiently do processing on our data. Pandas and DataFrame was efficient in processing the data for a certain kind of sort of task, but then we're going to see a more efficient language today. Video: 1: lec20-demo.i ... - JupyterLab

leaf_chunk.audio: So therefore, what we have had so far is not going to be scalable and we need to have different new mechanisms in order to process large amounts of data. What you also saw every time it gave you a data, we did not update the data, right? So the data was the static. So that is very important for you to decide what kind of technology you want to use in order to load the data and read the data. So we had the static data that was the small, but then in real life scenario, we have the data that is changing. So we have the snapshots with different frequency of the data and then the data set is going to be large. of those cases, we really want to move towards a technology that is really efficient in storing the data, and it can efficiently manage the data, and it can efficiently do processing on our data. Pandas and DataFrame was efficient in processing the data for a certain kind of sort of task, but then we're going to see a more efficient language today.

children.title: Data Management Systems and SQL Usage
children.summary: Data in real-world scenarios is typically stored in Database Management Systems (DBMS), which manage large datasets through various technologies. These systems facilitate data management and fulfill database requests. The lecture also introduces different types of data storage such as operational databases, data warehouses, and lakehouses, highlighting their characteristics like organization and size. Interaction with data is primarily done using Structured Query Language (SQL), which is essential for querying and managing the data effectively.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So in real world scenario, what I talked about was that we generally have large amount of data, and we have different technologies and different kinds of management systems or data management systems that sits on top of those data sets or tables in order to manage the data for us, and in order to fulfill the request that we have for the database. children.title: Operational Database Systems Overview
children.summary: Operational database systems are designed to store the day-to-day business data of various industries, such as user login information for social media platforms. These databases contain the latest data, which is frequently updated, ensuring that any changes, like password modifications, are immediately reflected. The data is typically small and organized, as it supports the operational functions of a business, allowing for efficient interaction with the data using Structured Query Language (SQL).

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So in the left side of this stream, we have operational database systems. operational database systems are databases that store day-to-day business of different industry. Like for example, if you are thinking about the login user name and password to your favorite social social media platform, and that's social media platform, that is stored in the operational database system that they have. Usually what we're thinking about is that the data is going to be The latest data, it will be updated frequently, imagine that you might want to change your password and immediately the change should be reflected in the database. But the dataset is also small and organized. So it is the operation of the entire business. So it should be super clean and organized and it should also be small, meaning that anything that the the user it interacts with at the various kind of the high level thing is going to be stored there. children.title: Introduction to SQL and Database Management Systems
children.summary: The segment introduces SQL as the main programming language for database management systems, discussing data storage methods like data warehouses, data lakes, and the hybrid Lakehouse model, along with their organizational differences. It defines the role of Database Management Systems (DBMS) in managing organized data, highlighting the necessity of SQL queries for cleaning and organizing large datasets. Access control in DBMS is covered, focusing on data integrity and user permissions. The history of database technology and common database iconography are also briefly mentioned. Additionally, SQL syntax is introduced, specifically the 'SELECT' statement for retrieving data, and the use of SQLAlchemy for database connections is demonstrated. The segment emphasizes formatting SQL queries for clarity and the importance of semicolons, along with using Pandas' 'pd.read_sql' function to execute queries and load data into DataFrames.

children.title: Introduction to SQL and Database Management Systems
children.summary: The lecture introduces SQL as the primary programming language for database management systems, discussing various data storage methods including data warehouses, data lakes, and the hybrid Lakehouse model. It explains the differences in organization and data handling among these systems, with operational data categorized by size and structure. The role of Database Management Systems (DBMS) in managing and accessing organized data is defined, emphasizing the need for SQL queries to clean and organize large datasets. The segment also covers access control in DBMS, detailing how they maintain data integrity and manage user permissions. Additionally, it touches on the history of database technology and the common iconography associated with databases.

children.title: Introduction to SQL and Data Storage Types
children.summary: The programming language for database management systems will be SQL, which is the focus of the lecture. Various methods of data storage and management are discussed, including data warehouses, data lakes, and the hybrid 'Lakehouse' model. These systems differ in organization and data handling, with operational data categorized by size and structure. Data is commonly stored in Database Management Systems, with technologies like Lakehouse providing snapshots of data over time, updated less frequently than operational databases. Data organization varies from small, structured tables to large, unstructured datasets, with interaction typically done using SQL.

children.title: Introduction to SQL and Data Storage Types
children.summary: The programming language for database management systems will be SQL, which will be the focus of today's lecture. Various methods of data storage and management are discussed, including data warehouses, data lakes, and a hybrid model known as a 'Lakehouse.' These systems differ in organization and data handling, with operational data being categorized based on size and structure.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: The programming language that we're going to use for all of these kinds of database management systems is going to be SQL. That's why we're going to learn SQL in today's lecture. There are also other different ways of storing the data and managing the data. So in the right hand side of the stream we have data warehouses, we also have data lakes, and then we also have have somewhere in between that uses Lake and Warehouse together. children.title: Overview of Data Storage Technologies
children.summary: Data is commonly stored in Database Management Systems, with various technologies like Lakehouse providing snapshots of data over time. These updates occur less frequently than in operational database systems. Data can be organized in tables for direct use, ranging from small and organized to large and messy datasets. Interaction with this data is typically done using Structured Query Language (SQL).

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And there is the technology that's called Lakehouse. You don't need to know the details of each one of them. But the only thing that you need to know is that every time that you're referring to any of these technologies, effectively, what's happening is that we have some data that is a snapshot in time. But the frequency is going to be probably less frequent updates than what we have in the operational database systems. Sometimes the data is organized and clean in tables, so you can directly use it. children.title: Data Management and Database Concepts Overview
children.summary: Data in real-world scenarios can be large and messy, requiring SQL queries and coding for cleaning and organization in Lake Houses. A database is defined as an organized collection of data, structured in various formats and managed by a Database Management System (DBMS), which acts as an interface for user interaction with the database, a structured repository of data.

children.title: Data Management and Database Concepts
children.summary: In real-world scenarios, data can be large and messy, necessitating the use of SQL queries and coding to clean and organize it for processing in Lake Houses. A database is defined as an organized collection of data, which can be structured in various formats, typically managed by a Database Management System (DBMS). The DBMS serves as an interface for users to interact with the database, which itself is not software but a structured repository of data.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Sometimes it is going to be, the data is going to be very large and it's going to be messy, meaning that you would need to write SQL queries and then write code in order to clean the data, put them in Lake Houses, and then you will deal with the clean data, which can still be large, but it just needs to be clean in a tablet or format, in a proper format for us to be able to process. So that's what's happening in general in real-world scenarios, which as you can imagine. There are different flavors and these different flavors would require different technologies for us to handle that. So what is a database and database is made up of two separate pieces. The database itself is an organized collection of data. So I want to specify how I want to organize my data. I would define whether my data is going to be in a tabular format or what not, and then I would restore my data in something that's called database. Database by itself is not a software, but there is a software that sits on top of the database that will manage the database and will be an interface for us to communicate to that database. children.title: Overview and Access Control in DBMS
children.summary: Database Management Systems (DBMS) are software systems that store, manage, and provide access to databases, ensuring data conforms to required formats and maintaining data integrity. They also facilitate controlled access to data, allowing users to manage permissions for specific entries. Examples of major DBMS include Google BigQuery, Amazon Redshift, and Oracle, which utilize variations of SQL as the programming language essential for data interaction.

children.title: Overview of Database Management Systems (DBMS)
children.summary: Database Management Systems (DBMS) are software systems designed to store, manage, and facilitate access to databases. They serve as an interface for data storage, ensuring that the data conforms to the required format of the underlying database. This includes managing updates and maintaining data integrity.

children.title: Database Management Systems and Access Control
children.summary: Database Management Systems (DBMS) facilitate controlled access to data, allowing users to manage permissions for specific data entries. Major DBMS examples include Google BigQuery, Amazon Redshift, and Oracle, which utilize SQL as a common programming language, albeit with different variations. Understanding SQL is essential for interacting with these systems, as it serves as the interface for data access.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And it can also facilitate access, meaning that I can actually use the BMS system in order to make sure that you only have access to the rows width, for example, or the entries with the data 100 annotation kind of thing. And that's the way that permission is going to be controlled using database management systems, but then the entire enterprise or one industry is going to store the entire data set or in all of the databases, but they just need to manage access using the DBMS software that sits on top of that. There are really famous, largest scale DBMS systems. The names you might have heard some of these names, Google BigQuery, Amazon Redshift. There is data bricks, there's snowflake, and all of these new, also technologies that we're working on. You might have heard about Oracle. Oracle is a database management system that usually institutions use in order to process the data. But just know that it's a software that sits on top of your data and you're interacting with that software and that interface in order to get proper access to the data. What they do is actually under the hood, they all share the same programming language. They all speak in the SQL language, but they might use different flavors of SQL. So that's why we're going to use SQL that independent of the technology that they're going to that that one enterprise is going to use. use under the hood if you want to speak to these DBMS softwares you need to speak within the SQL language. So, I have one slide for you for you to think about where this technology actually comes from. Video: 1: lec20-demo.i ... - JupyterLab

leaf_chunk.audio: And it can also facilitate access, meaning that I can actually use the BMS system in order to make sure that you only have access to the rows width, for example, or the entries with the data 100 annotation kind of thing. And that's the way that permission is going to be controlled using database management systems, but then the entire enterprise or one industry is going to store the entire data set or in all of the databases, but they just need to manage access using the DBMS software that sits on top of that. There are really famous, largest scale DBMS systems. The names you might have heard some of these names, Google BigQuery, Amazon Redshift. There is data bricks, there's snowflake, and all of these new, also technologies that we're working on. You might have heard about Oracle. Oracle is a database management system that usually institutions use in order to process the data. But just know that it's a software that sits on top of your data and you're interacting with that software and that interface in order to get proper access to the data. What they do is actually under the hood, they all share the same programming language. They all speak in the SQL language, but they might use different flavors of SQL. So that's why we're going to use SQL that independent of the technology that they're going to that that one enterprise is going to use. use under the hood if you want to speak to these DBMS softwares you need to speak within the SQL language. So, I have one slide for you for you to think about where this technology actually comes from.

children.title: Database Icons, Technology History, and DBMS Advantages
children.summary: The segment explores the common icon used to represent databases, with participants suggesting various interpretations such as multi-level storage containers and disk drives. It then traces the history of database technology, highlighting the evolution from early hard disk drives to instantaneous transaction processing, referencing the first IBM commercial disk with a capacity of five megabytes. The advantages of Database Management Systems (DBMS) are discussed, noting their reliable data storage, backup capabilities, and efficiency in managing large datasets compared to traditional methods. Finally, course recommendations are provided, detailing CS86, which covers database design and performance, and Data 101, focusing on applied data engineering, with offerings in spring and fall respectively.

children.title: Icon Representation of Databases
children.summary: The segment discusses the common icon used to represent databases, prompting participants to identify what the icon depicts. Various suggestions are offered, including multi-level storage containers, stacks of weights, rows of data, disk drives, and other storage-related imagery.

children.title: History of Database Technology and Transaction Processing
children.summary: The discussion covers the evolution of database technology, starting from early hard disk drives that used flat disks for data storage. It explains the mechanics of these drives, including the use of magnetic disks and read heads that process data encoded in binary. A historical reference is made to the first IBM commercial disk, which had a capacity of five megabytes. The lecture emphasizes the shift from batch processing of transactions to instantaneous transaction processing, highlighting the changing definition of 'instantaneous' over time.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So the technology of the database or the technology that we're going to talk about today is kind of an ancient technology and then it starts from the time that. People use hard disk and different disk drives in order to store the data and then the size or the capacity of all of these hard drives. We're pretty much a small and then the way that the data was restored was in different flat disks And those disks are kind of magnets and there's always kind of the head that's going to Read the data from all of those rotating disks that are going to Rotate so I'm gonna move and show you this one So I don't know if you've seen it you might be too young to have seen these kind of technology But again, what's happening is that we have all of these disk drives And then there's also a driver that actually is going to read one line of data all is coded in zero and once. And then as the disc rotates is going to read all of those information. So the entire kind of when we're thinking about the stack of the data or the stack of all of these describes. This is coming from the first IBM commercial described. So it has the capacity of five megabytes, and it was a big deal back then. So you can think about all of these describes, which are kind of glass layers that has metal framing with the head that's actually going to read the data off of this entire commercial described system that we have. The idea was that they wanted to make sure that every time that the transaction is going to happen, the transaction is going to happen instantly, and instantly has a different definition now compared to back then. And then they just wanted to move away from just... Processing all of the transactions in batches because they would collect all of the transactions in batch and then they would run one batch of all transactions they wanted to not do that and every time that there is a transaction they go ahead and process that an updated database or write the data into the database and so on and so forth. So this is exactly kind of where this icon is coming from in some of the different. a kind of illustrations you might see a picture of elephant and someone guess why database is shown with an elephant Elephant has the best memory. So because we're storing all of the data in a database and that's the source of the data that we're using. So sometimes it is also shown with an elephant icon. Video: 1: lec20-demo.i ... - JupyterLab

leaf_chunk.audio: So the technology of the database or the technology that we're going to talk about today is kind of an ancient technology and then it starts from the time that. People use hard disk and different disk drives in order to store the data and then the size or the capacity of all of these hard drives. We're pretty much a small and then the way that the data was restored was in different flat disks And those disks are kind of magnets and there's always kind of the head that's going to Read the data from all of those rotating disks that are going to Rotate so I'm gonna move and show you this one So I don't know if you've seen it you might be too young to have seen these kind of technology But again, what's happening is that we have all of these disk drives And then there's also a driver that actually is going to read one line of data all is coded in zero and once. And then as the disc rotates is going to read all of those information. So the entire kind of when we're thinking about the stack of the data or the stack of all of these describes. This is coming from the first IBM commercial described. So it has the capacity of five megabytes, and it was a big deal back then. So you can think about all of these describes, which are kind of glass layers that has metal framing with the head that's actually going to read the data off of this entire commercial described system that we have. The idea was that they wanted to make sure that every time that the transaction is going to happen, the transaction is going to happen instantly, and instantly has a different definition now compared to back then. And then they just wanted to move away from just... Processing all of the transactions in batches because they would collect all of the transactions in batch and then they would run one batch of all transactions they wanted to not do that and every time that there is a transaction they go ahead and process that an updated database or write the data into the database and so on and so forth. So this is exactly kind of where this icon is coming from in some of the different. a kind of illustrations you might see a picture of elephant and someone guess why database is shown with an elephant Elephant has the best memory. So because we're storing all of the data in a database and that's the source of the data that we're using. So sometimes it is also shown with an elephant icon.

children.title: Advantages of Database Management Systems
children.summary: Database Management Systems (DBMS) provide reliable data storage with backup capabilities, protecting against data loss from system failures, unlike traditional storage methods like CSV files or local drives. DBMS allows for external computation, enabling the processing of large datasets that cannot be handled locally. Additionally, DBMS are designed to enhance performance by efficiently managing data retrieval, sorting, and processing based on the data structure, ensuring optimal task execution.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So let me talk a little bit about some of the advantages of using database over some CSV files that we have been using before. What we are doing with the DBMS software systems is that we have reliable storage. If there is any failure in the system, if your data wipe start for whatever reason, there is a backup in the database management system that can backup your system. We don't have that with your laptops or we don't have that with the old disk drives. If there is some crash, if something is happening, there's no software that can retrieve your data in a reliable way. There are other considerations that we can do the compute in the using the dbms that sit somewhere in the cloud or on some kind of server as opposed to our own computers. So if the data is so large that you can train a model locally or it can process the data locally, what you would do is you would write the query, it will be run externally and the result of that is going to be returned to you, which is really important. Speaking of handling more data, what's happening with the database systems is that they are designed to improve performance, meaning that they are going, if you say that, oh, I want this database, return this table to me, it will return it to you in order that is more efficient. If you want to ask it to sort the data, if you want to ask it to randomize the data, if any kind of processing that you want to do, the DBMS decides based on the structure of your data, how you can do that more efficiently. There's no uniform or a certain way that the DBS is going to manage a certain task that you have, but it is more like the best design of the DBS systems are going to actually be designed in a way that it improves performance. leaf_chunk.audio: So let me talk a little bit about some of the advantages of using database over some CSV files that we have been using before. What we are doing with the DBMS software systems is that we have reliable storage. If there is any failure in the system, if your data wipe start for whatever reason, there is a backup in the database management system that can backup your system. We don't have that with your laptops or we don't have that with the old disk drives. If there is some crash, if something is happening, there's no software that can retrieve your data in a reliable way. There are other considerations that we can do the compute in the using the dbms that sit somewhere in the cloud or on some kind of server as opposed to our own computers. So if the data is so large that you can train a model locally or it can process the data locally, what you would do is you would write the query, it will be run externally and the result of that is going to be returned to you, which is really important. Speaking of handling more data, what's happening with the database systems is that they are designed to improve performance, meaning that they are going, if you say that, oh, I want this database, return this table to me, it will return it to you in order that is more efficient. If you want to ask it to sort the data, if you want to ask it to randomize the data, if any kind of processing that you want to do, the DBMS decides based on the structure of your data, how you can do that more efficiently. There's no uniform or a certain way that the DBS is going to manage a certain task that you have, but it is more like the best design of the DBS systems are going to actually be designed in a way that it improves performance.

children.title: Course Recommendations: CS86 and Data Engineering
children.summary: CS86 is a databases course that covers database design, reliability, fault tolerance, and performance considerations. Data 101, offered by the data science department, focuses on applied data engineering, including various DBMS systems and handling big data. CS86 is typically offered in the spring, while Data 101 is usually available in the fall.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: We also have a really good course in CS. It's called CS86, that's databases course, which not only teaches you databases, but it also teaches you the design of databases. so that you have all of their reliability, fault tolerance, and all of the performance considerations that you have. We also have data 101 that is called data, the title of the course is data engineering offered from the data science department. It's more applied so it will show you different kind of DBMS systems and how to use them and how to handle big data. So that's also a very useful course for you to consider. This is usually offered in the fall. The CS186 is usually offered in the spring semester. Video: 1: lec20-demo.i ... - JupyterLab

children.title: Importance of Database Management Systems (DBMS)
children.summary: Database Management Systems (DBMS) are crucial for logically organizing data and enforcing constraints that ensure data integrity, unlike raw data formats such as CSV, where random or null values can be entered without control. DBMS also facilitates access management, allowing permissions to be configured for different users accessing various tables in the database. Additionally, DBMS provides reliable storage that can withstand system crashes and optimizes data computation, even for data that exceeds memory capacity.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So what do I mean by data management and we said that dbms stands for database management system. What do we refer to in terms of management and why is it important? We want to make sure that the data is logically organized and it is organized in a way that with the constraints that we're also is specifying for our... for our... or for our data set. We could not actually enforce kind of constraints in data frame because someone could come in and enter random value or enter, for example, a null value without us being able to have any kind of control over that. So this will help us to logically organize the data and also manage the access so we can give access permission access to different people accessing different types of tables that we have in our database. children.title: Advantages of DBMS over Raw Files
children.summary: Database Management Systems (DBMS) provide reliable storage that can survive system crashes and disk failures, ensuring data integrity. They optimize computations on data that exceeds memory capacity and utilize special data structures to enhance performance. Additionally, DBMS allows for logical data organization and access control, which is crucial for managing constantly changing enterprise data, unlike static datasets typically provided in coursework.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: It can enforce guarantee on the data. Again, when you're enforcing some kind of a requirement that, oh, you can add non-negative for some, for a person's weight, or you can, you cannot add no values. It will, when you have that data, it ensures that some minimum requirement in your data is met. So, EDA is going to be easier for those databases. It prevents data anomaly and then it is also going to let us do So it's safe concurrent operation. So this is very important. Remember that I was saying that the data set that we usually give you is a static and like we don't change the data set, like we give you MPG data set, you would just directly use it as a, as we won't go and change the data. But in the databases, in general enterprise, the data is constantly changing. Video: 1: lec20-demo.i ... - JupyterLab

Remember that I was saying that the data set that we usually give you is a static and like we don't change the data set, like we give you MPG data set, you would just directly use it as a, as we won't go and change the data. children.title: Advantages of DBMS Over Raw Files
children.summary: Database Management Systems (DBMS) provide reliable data storage that can withstand system crashes and disk failures, optimizing computation for data that exceeds memory capacity. They offer structured data management, allowing configuration of data organization and access control. DBMS can enforce data integrity guarantees, such as ensuring non-negative values for attributes like weight or age, and help prevent data anomalies. Additionally, they support safe concurrent operations, enabling multiple users to read and write data simultaneously, as seen in scenarios like ATM transactions.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So for example, if you go to a bank and then you withdraw money and you want to deposit money in the same account, If it cannot or vice versa, if the system fails all of a sudden, you might lose the money that you actually didn't withdraw. So you don't have it in cash. It was supposed to be transferred from one account to another account. Video: 1: lec20-demo.i ... - JupyterLab

children.title: Database Transactions and Concurrent Operations
children.summary: In database systems, operations are executed as transactions, which must be completed in their entirety or not at all, ensuring safety and reliability. This is crucial for concurrent operations, such as those in login systems, allowing multiple users to read and write data simultaneously without issues. The upcoming demo will illustrate these concepts, emphasizing that users typically interact with databases via programming languages rather than directly on the server.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So the data is withdrawn, but then or the money is withdrawn, but it's not deposited into the next account. So you might lose that money. So what's happening in database systems is that they say that all of the operations are being done as transaction. So you define a transaction, the entire transaction is either going to be completed or not completed. It cannot be interrupted in the middle. So that's why it is very safe to do concurrent operations. Again, also for security for login systems and all of those things, this kind of concurrent operations is really important for us. So go into the demo quickly in the next kind of few slides, but I also want to give you an intuition of what's going to happen when we go to the demo. So generally when you're working with a database, you're working on your on computer. You're not going to move and like work on that actual server or something. So you're working on your computer. Usually you're working on it using a different programming language than SQL. Video: 1: lec20-demo.i ... - JupyterLab

leaf_chunk.audio: So the data is withdrawn, but then or the money is withdrawn, but it's not deposited into the next account. So you might lose that money. So what's happening in database systems is that they say that all of the operations are being done as transaction. So you define a transaction, the entire transaction is either going to be completed or not completed. It cannot be interrupted in the middle. So that's why it is very safe to do concurrent operations. Again, also for security for login systems and all of those things, this kind of concurrent operations is really important for us. So go into the demo quickly in the next kind of few slides, but I also want to give you an intuition of what's going to happen when we go to the demo. So generally when you're working with a database, you're working on your on computer. You're not going to move and like work on that actual server or something. So you're working on your computer. Usually you're working on it using a different programming language than SQL.

children.title: Using Python with SQL for Data Management and DBMS Interaction
children.summary: Python is utilized to access SQL, which is typically managed within another programming language, with data organized into tables to minimize redundancy. Students will learn to structure data effectively in core database courses. Different database types, such as product, sales, and customer information tables, illustrate the separation of responsibilities in data management. Users interact with a DBMS by writing SQL queries, which return data in table format. The segment also discusses the interaction between a website and a centralized DBMS, emphasizing the need for seamless user experience while querying user-related information. It covers redundancy and resource allocation, detailing how different resources communicate with the DBMS, prioritizing the web server's access to specific portions of the DBMS.

children.title: Using Python with SQL for Data Management
children.summary: Python is often used to access SQL, which is typically managed within another programming language. Data is organized into tables for various purposes, emphasizing the importance of separating responsibilities and minimizing redundancy. In core database courses, students will learn how to structure data into different tables and sources effectively.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So today we're going to see Python because usually SQL is going to be a language that we access within another programming language because usually the software is managed by something else like Python and then within that we're going to run SQL queries. And then what we're going to do is that we have stored our data into some tables. So if you take a look at here, we have some tables here, some table here, and some table here. These are the data that is organized for different purposes. Again, in core database courses, you're going to learn how to organize the data into different tables, into different data sources, So that there is separation of responsibilities between the tables. There is limited amount of redundancy between the tables that is organized properly. Video: 1: lec20-demo.i ... - JupyterLab

leaf_chunk.audio: So today we're going to see Python because usually SQL is going to be a language that we access within another programming language because usually the software is managed by something else like Python and then within that we're going to run SQL queries. And then what we're going to do is that we have stored our data into some tables. So if you take a look at here, we have some tables here, some table here, and some table here. These are the data that is organized for different purposes. Again, in core database courses, you're going to learn how to organize the data into different tables, into different data sources, So that there is separation of responsibilities between the tables. There is limited amount of redundancy between the tables that is organized properly.

children.title: Interacting with a DBMS and SQL Queries
children.summary: Different types of databases, such as product, sales, and customer information tables, illustrate the separation of responsibilities in data management. A DBMS server manages the databases, and users interact with it by writing SQL queries. SQL is a table-centric language, meaning all data returned is in table format, regardless of whether it consists of single columns or multiple data types. Transactions also yield tables, contrasting with data structures like data frames and series used in pandas.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So for example, here I have three different kind of databases or three different kinds of tables that I've restored. One is product, one is sale, and then sales one is customer information, for example. So you can see that there is separation of responsibilities between them. But what manages the entire database is this DBMS server. So you're always talking to a server or some kind of a management system, or you're connecting to them in order to get the data. What we would do on our computer is we would write query. When I say SQL query, that means that I need to speak to the language that the DBMS system or server understands. I would send the query, It will process it on the server and then it will return the data to me in terms of a format, in terms of a table. So SQL is a table language. Everything that you're going to get is a table. Even if that's single, rather than if that's a single column, we're not going to have different versions of data types. Like in pandas, we had data frame, we had series, we have index, and all of those things. Here, everything is going to be table. You do a transaction, you get a table. leaf_chunk.audio: So for example, here I have three different kind of databases or three different kinds of tables that I've restored. One is product, one is sale, and then sales one is customer information, for example. So you can see that there is separation of responsibilities between them. But what manages the entire database is this DBMS server. So you're always talking to a server or some kind of a management system, or you're connecting to them in order to get the data. What we would do on our computer is we would write query. When I say SQL query, that means that I need to speak to the language that the DBMS system or server understands. I would send the query, It will process it on the server and then it will return the data to me in terms of a format, in terms of a table. So SQL is a table language. Everything that you're going to get is a table. Even if that's single, rather than if that's a single column, we're not going to have different versions of data types. Like in pandas, we had data frame, we had series, we have index, and all of those things. Here, everything is going to be table. You do a transaction, you get a table.

children.title: Interacting with a DBMS and Resource Allocation
children.summary: The segment discusses the interaction between an enterprise's website and a centralized database management system (DBMS). It highlights the necessity for the website to query the DBMS for user-related information, such as product details and user credentials, emphasizing the importance of maintaining a seamless user experience. Additionally, it covers the concepts of redundancy and resource allocation, explaining how different resources communicate with the DBMS without interfering with each other's processes. The web server is prioritized in this communication, allowing it to access a specific portion of the DBMS, while other services for data engineers and data scientists are managed separately.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: you store a table and so on and so forth. That's how our relationship with a centralized database that multiple different systems are connecting to that. So imagine that an enterprise is going to have their website, the website is going to actually need to talk to the DBMS server. It needs to, for example, get some information about like, if the user is browsing some products, it needs to make it query, get the products displayed at, or it needs to make sure that it can recognize the user credentials so that it lets the user log in and whatnot. So there is that kind of connection there that you cannot interrupt, because that would interrupt user experience. In reality, there is a lot of different work going on in terms of how to make redundancy and how to make resource allocations that at once different resources are going to talk to the DBMS server, they're not going to help each other's processes. And then we prioritize based on the priority of different tasks that we have. So probably in this case, Web server gets the highest priority. It talks to a separate portion of DBMS server, and then the rest of the DBMS server is going to serve the rest of the services that we have here for the data engineer, for data scientists, and so on and so forth. leaf_chunk.audio: you store a table and so on and so forth. That's how our relationship with a centralized database that multiple different systems are connecting to that. So imagine that an enterprise is going to have their website, the website is going to actually need to talk to the DBMS server. It needs to, for example, get some information about like, if the user is browsing some products, it needs to make it query, get the products displayed at, or it needs to make sure that it can recognize the user credentials so that it lets the user log in and whatnot. So there is that kind of connection there that you cannot interrupt, because that would interrupt user experience. In reality, there is a lot of different work going on in terms of how to make redundancy and how to make resource allocations that at once different resources are going to talk to the DBMS server, they're not going to help each other's processes. And then we prioritize based on the priority of different tasks that we have. So probably in this case, Web server gets the highest priority. It talks to a separate portion of DBMS server, and then the rest of the DBMS server is going to serve the rest of the services that we have here for the data engineer, for data scientists, and so on and so forth.

children.title: Introduction to SQL: Structure and Importance
children.summary: Structured Query Language (SQL) is defined as a programming language with a specific structure for writing queries, designed to enforce organization in databases. It is distinct from general-purpose programming languages like Python or Java, serving a specialized role in managing and communicating with database systems. SQL is essential for handling large datasets and is the dominant language in the industry, making its understanding crucial for effective data processing and modeling in real-world applications.

children.title: Introduction to SQL and Its Structure
children.summary: SQL, or Structured Query Language, is defined as a programming language that follows a specific structure for writing queries. It is designed to enforce a certain structure in databases that store data. SQL allows users to write queries to request data, which is then fulfilled and returned by the database.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So let's go into just seeing what SQL is and then we'll start writing queries. So what is SQL? I say SQL or SQL. It stands for structured query language. So I'm going to highlight that it is a structured because you're going to see that it follows a very specific structure of writing different queries. And then that's also how it's going to enforce a certain structure in the databases that is restoring the data. It is a query language because we just write the query, we actually send the query and then we get an query of requests and then someone else is going to fulfill their request. And then it's going to return back to us the data that we want and language is just this is that this is separate programming language. Video: 1: lec20-demo.i ... - JupyterLab

leaf_chunk.audio: So let's go into just seeing what SQL is and then we'll start writing queries. So what is SQL? I say SQL or SQL. It stands for structured query language. So I'm going to highlight that it is a structured because you're going to see that it follows a very specific structure of writing different queries. And then that's also how it's going to enforce a certain structure in the databases that is restoring the data. It is a query language because we just write the query, we actually send the query and then we get an query of requests and then someone else is going to fulfill their request. And then it's going to return back to us the data that we want and language is just this is that this is separate programming language.

children.title: Introduction to SQL as a Programming Language
children.summary: Structured Query Language (SQL) is introduced as a distinct programming language specifically designed for managing and communicating with database systems. Unlike general-purpose programming languages such as Python, Java, or C++, SQL serves a special purpose and is often utilized within these languages to perform database operations.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: own programming language as I said, it is distinct from other programming languages, but we usually call it within another programming language. Remember in the previous picture that I showed you, usually work in notebook, coding in pandas or Python, and then you're going to mix it with different SQL kind of commands in order to get the data that you want in the right format. It's this This is a special purpose programming language. It is specifically designed to manage different databases. You cannot use it for web design or different things. It has a special purpose. As opposed to, for example, Python, Java, C++, that it can be used for different purposes. Video: 1: lec20-demo.i ... - JupyterLab

children.title: Introduction to SQL and Its Importance
children.summary: Structured Query Language (SQL) is a distinct programming language used for communicating with database systems, essential for working with large datasets. It is important to learn SQL as it is the dominant language in the industry, and while it can be called from other languages like Python, it serves a specific purpose in data management. Understanding SQL is crucial for data processing and modeling in real-world scenarios.

Audio: This is not going to be like that. It is a dominant language for working with the data. Period. And that's why you should know it. That's why we're going to cover it in data 100. First of all, their data is going to be large. Second of all, you cannot expect data engineers to actually do the entire processing with for you. children.title: Introduction to SQL and Its Applications
children.summary: Structured Query Language (SQL) is a programming language used for communication with database systems, primarily structured as tables. It is vital for managing and processing data effectively and was originally known as 'Structured English Query Language' when introduced in the 1970s. SQL serves as a common language across various database management systems (DBMS), though each may have unique variations. Understanding core SQL principles is crucial for adapting to specific DBMS nuances. Additionally, SQL can be integrated with Jupyter Notebook using a library that simulates server connections, with SQLite being introduced as a simplified version for quick database interactions, particularly useful for small applications and mobile devices.

children.title: Introduction to SQL and Its Importance
children.summary: Structured Query Language (SQL) is a distinct programming language used for communication with database systems, often called from within other programming languages like Python. It is a dominant technology for working with data, and all inputs and outputs in SQL are structured as tables. Understanding SQL is essential for managing and processing data effectively.

children.title: Introduction to SQL and Database Management Systems
children.summary: Structured Query Language (SQL) is a distinct programming language used for database communication, introduced in the 1970s as 'Structured English Query Language.' It operates primarily with tables, making it essential for data handling. While SQL serves as a common language across various database management systems (DBMS), each system may have unique variations. Students are encouraged to grasp the core principles of SQL, allowing them to adapt to specific DBMS nuances in different enterprises.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Back then, it was referred to as a structured English query of language or SQL like this. That's probably why we're also going to read SQL as SQL when we're talking about the this is specific topic of today. Most systems don't follow the same standard. There is just a common ground of like, what are different commands that we have in SQL language, but most different database management systems have different, slight different flavors of it. So expect that you will learn the core knowledge, but then you have to learn different specific details and nitty gritty details of each one of the DBMS systems that you're gonna work with. That's why it is okay if you change enterprise, or if you are new to an enterprise to just give yourself time to learn about DBMS, as long as you just know that underlying general logic of working with SQL. children.title: Introduction to SQL and Jupyter Notebook Integration
children.summary: SQL will be used in Jupyter Notebook to connect to databases, specifically utilizing a library that simulates server connections without needing a separate server. SQLite, a simplified version of SQL, will be introduced as a programming library that can be imported directly into the notebook for quick database interactions, commonly used for small applications and mobile devices. SQL is a distinct programming language for database communication, essential for data work, and was originally called 'Structured English Query Language'.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: What we're going to use is we're going to program in SQL using Jupyter Notebook. We're going to have different versions of. how we can connect to our database and then we're going to actually use doc DB this semester in order to actually make your connection and then it's a library that kind of simulates that server connection that we don't want to actually build a separate server. So it is kind of a simulation of this real-life scenario that you might see in industry, but we're going to use it on notebook on your actual Jupyter Notebooks, Jupyter Notebook platform. There are different kinds of In notebook or like on your computer ways that you can actually simulate how your code can speak to a database. So there is kind of a DBMS system that's called SQLite. SQLite is a simplified version of SQL. It is actually a programming library. So we can directly go into the notebook, import that programming library as SQLite. And then we are going to be able to do quick and quick stuff, essentially. This SQL Lite is commonly used for small apps and mobile devices. Video: 1: lec20-demo.i ... - JupyterLab

leaf_chunk.audio: What we're going to use is we're going to program in SQL using Jupyter Notebook. We're going to have different versions of. how we can connect to our database and then we're going to actually use doc DB this semester in order to actually make your connection and then it's a library that kind of simulates that server connection that we don't want to actually build a separate server. So it is kind of a simulation of this real-life scenario that you might see in industry, but we're going to use it on notebook on your actual Jupyter Notebooks, Jupyter Notebook platform. There are different kinds of In notebook or like on your computer ways that you can actually simulate how your code can speak to a database. So there is kind of a DBMS system that's called SQLite. SQLite is a simplified version of SQL. It is actually a programming library. So we can directly go into the notebook, import that programming library as SQLite. And then we are going to be able to do quick and quick stuff, essentially. This SQL Lite is commonly used for small apps and mobile devices.

children.title: Introduction to SQLite, DuckDB, and Jupyter SQL
children.summary: SQLite is introduced as a user-friendly library for manipulating database files or in-memory databases with a simplified SQL, ideal for small applications, especially on mobile devices. DuckDB is highlighted for its comprehensive SQL capabilities, suitable for data analysis on large datasets, prompting a transition in the course to focus on DuckDB. Jupyter magic commands, specifically 'load_ext sql', enable SQL functionalities in Jupyter notebooks. The segment also covers connecting to database files, demonstrating commands for SQLite and DuckDB in JupyterLab, and discusses connecting to enterprise data platforms like Snowflake and Databricks using SQLAlchemy.

children.title: Introduction to SQLite and Doctybe
children.summary: SQLite is introduced as an easy-to-use library for directly manipulating database files or in-memory databases using a simplified version of SQL. It is commonly used for small applications, particularly on mobile devices, due to its optimization for simplicity and speed in handling basic data tasks like lookups and record additions. The course will not focus on SQL but will teach Doctybe, which is similar to SQL with additional functionalities.

children.title: Introduction to SQLite and DuckDB in Data100
children.summary: SQLite is a library that allows direct manipulation of database files or in-memory databases using a simplified version of SQL, commonly used for small mobile apps. DuckDB, on the other hand, provides a more complete SQL experience and can manipulate database files, CSV files, or in-memory pandas dataframes, making it increasingly popular for data analysis on large datasets. The course will be transitioning to DuckDB for Data100 due to its optimization for advanced data analysis tasks.

children.title: Using Jupyter Magic for SQL Extensions
children.summary: Jupyter magic allows users to execute commands in a command line interface format within Jupyter notebooks. To utilize SQL functionalities, the command 'load_ext sql' is used to load the SQL extension, enabling access to SQL features in the notebook.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So let's move on to the demo and then see A few things. Now that we have the logistics of why you should learn SQL out of the way, now we're up to the interesting part. So the interesting part is that there is some notion in Jupyter, which is called Jupyter magic, that every time that you have percent and you have some command, you're effectively telling Jupyter that I have this command and I want you to run this command as if this is a command line interface. And what we're doing here is that we're saying that load the command or run the command load underscore X, E X t stands for loading an extension and the extension that we're loading is SQL. This will give us access to all of the SQL functionalities. So that's what we're going to do first every time that you want to use SQL in the notebook. Video: 1: lec20-demo.i ... - JupyterLab

[ ]: pip install jupysql -- upgrade

1. Load the sql Module.

[ ]: %load_ext sql
2. Connect to a database.
Here, we connect to the SQLite database basic_examples.db and DuckDB database example_duck.db .

leaf_chunk.audio: So let's move on to the demo and then see A few things. Now that we have the logistics of why you should learn SQL out of the way, now we're up to the interesting part. So the interesting part is that there is some notion in Jupyter, which is called Jupyter magic, that every time that you have percent and you have some command, you're effectively telling Jupyter that I have this command and I want you to run this command as if this is a command line interface. And what we're doing here is that we're saying that load the command or run the command load underscore X, E X t stands for loading an extension and the extension that we're loading is SQL. This will give us access to all of the SQL functionalities. So that's what we're going to do first every time that you want to use SQL in the notebook.
